headlineSummary: Anthropic drew notably negative feedback about usage limits, pricing and product quality, while OpenAI also leaned negative on limited evidence and remains closely tied with Anthropic [E3][E4][E5][E2].

ranking.summary: Anthropic was mostly criticized for very tight usage limits, token-spending incentives, and slower or weaker model behavior, with one positive note about Claude Code helping with migrations; it is in a close tie with OpenAI [E1][E3][E4][E5][E6].
OpenAI's sentiment was negative on limited evidence, driven by a comparison saying GPT-5 looked worse than a cheaper competitor, and it is in a close tie with Anthropic [E2].
Microsoft Copilot was mildly negative because a comment framed the model swap and 7.5x modifier as a price hike for slower performance and a step backward [E6].

evidence.excerpt: Well: Claude Code migrated it all, sometimes rewriting parts when the libraries where no longer available. Today complex migrations are much simpler to perform, which, I believe, will increase the mobility across providers a lot.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly credits Claude Code with successfully handling a messy migration and says such migrations are now much simpler.

evidence.excerpt: GLM-5.1 is less than half the cost to complete the suite of GPT-5 and is dramatically more capable than both GPT-5 and o3.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt explicitly names GPT-5 and says a competitor is both much cheaper and dramatically more capable than GPT-5 and o3.

evidence.excerpt: I'd be ok with paying more if results were good, but it seems like Anthropic is going for the Tinder/casino intermittent reinforcement strategy: optimized to keep you spending tokens instead of achieving results.

annotations.rationale: The commenter directly accuses Anthropic of optimizing for token spending rather than user results, which is strong criticism of strategy and trustworthiness.

evidence.excerpt: After just ~4 prompts I blew past my daily limit. Another ~7 more prompts & I blew past my weekly limit. The entire HTMl/CSS/JS was less than 300 lines of code. I was shocked how fast it exhausted my usage limits.

annotations.rationale: The comment explicitly discusses Opus 4.7 usage limits and describes them as exhausting shockingly fast after very few prompts.

evidence.excerpt: Then I looked at the usage and it said I had used 95% of my Claude design usage for the week! This isn't a real tool. This is a plaything, if that's what they're providing as examples.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly refers to Claude Design, complains about extreme weekly usage consumption, and concludes it is not a real tool.

evidence.excerpt: But now I see that 4.7 is going to replace both 4.5 and 4.6 in VSCode Copilot, and with a 7.5x modifier. Based on the description, this is going to be a price hike for slower performance — and if the 4.5 to 4.6 change is any guide, more overthinking targeted at long-running tasks, rather than fine-grained. For me, that seems like a step backwards.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt criticizes Opus 4.7 as slower, more overthinking, and a step backward relative to earlier Opus versions.
The comment explicitly says VSCode Copilot will replace models with a 7.5x modifier and calls it a price hike for slower performance.
